speaker: interviewer
text: You wanna finish the story about-- [PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling a story about meeting a Polish doctor on the street that you knew from before.

speaker: interviewee
text: The one I said that we going to perish? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Well, let's-- why don't we move forward to your emigration?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emigration. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So, these boys, the two Americans, he went-- he-- they went back. And they told my family there-- whoever-- they told that we are survived, be it, the two of us. So they send us affidavits. And on the base of affidavits and the ground of affidavits, I could get a Polish passport. And then-- but I couldn't come here, because Stalin wouldn't let me come here-- only it opened up the-- Israel, and all I could leave for to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So. When did you leave for Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe-- maybe '47? Was it Palestine or Israel that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the fight was going on. The fight was going on. It wasn't yet Israel. It was sort of.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you go with?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a-- we were going with the organization. I think I forgot to tell you that when I was yet in Warsaw, there-- there was a Jewish in the military-- a Jewish colonel who-- a communist, which I wasn't in my lifetime. I didn't belong in my life to any party in the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: So news came to the-- my sister had a big mouth. And she was telling them what's going on-- what we trying to do-- that we have relatives. We're going to leave. And the Poles didn't want to know that part. So one Sunday morning, we are still in bed. Somebody's at the door, and gave me a invitation, a call, to go to another part of Warsaw, far away, and to be investigated or something-- whatever you want to call it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister knew that she the trouble maker-- that she caused me all this. If she wouldn't talk, we wouldn't have all this. She got scared. I go with you. I say, you don't have-- I want to go with you. She felt guilty what she did to me-- didn't have to talk. Here, take with you cookies. Take with you a napkin. And-- and we went there. And we came in front to a door where you had to wait. In the door--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had us wait. And the door had cushions. So you were waiting outside. You can't hear what they talking inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they called me in. And they-- he said, is that true that you want to leave Poland? I said, yes. What the reason? I said, no specific reason. I have relatives. Did anybody of the government abuse you, or offend you, or harm you in any way? No. I have nothing against the government-- all kinds of questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said, if that case, you free to go. That's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sister go to Israel with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She went two years before me. I didn't want to go at all. She went two years before me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go alone or with someone?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to Marseilles. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They took us, the-- who was married that time. I didn't. I wasn't. They took us into a train going to Marseilles. And them husbands, whoever had a-- they were not allowed to go underground because of politics.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were flying. And the wives and the husband met in Marseilles. In Marseilles, we stay-- stayed about 10 days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one Saturday night, it came about secretly from the Arabs-- because the British didn't want to let the Arabs get mad. So Saturday night came a little boat and took us to Italy, to-- I forgot the name. It's on the North of Italy. I forgot the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were there. Then we went to Paris. We stayed in Paris some days. And from Paris, we went to-- now we leaving Israel or going to Israel? Going to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Going to Israel. That Saturday night, we went to-- in Italy. I forgot the name. And we went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: By boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Military boat, where we were sitting on the floor. They had soldiers. They used to call it [NON-ENGLISH]-- that's dry bread. What do you call dry bread in English? No special name? It was provision for the soldiers. And dirty as it was, they had a-- a tub with tea in it-- and dirty as could be. And that's what we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get to Israel? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what is it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was very sick-- seasick. And I was lying there. And I knew a little Latin. And the Italian have their own language.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I was so sick and my head is hanging down, out the cart like this, because I'm so seasick-- and they felt sorry for me, these cooks or somebody-- the Italians-- and they brought the pea soup for me-- something to eat. I couldn't eat. But there was a cup of it, a little baby. And he looked at me-- or she-- I think the baby was. Now the boys, the Jewish boys-- I want to stick with you. So you got over being sick, and you got to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you in Israel? What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel, we came to the port, and they loaded us in buses. And they took us to the desert. I don't know name. I don't know. Do you know Pardes Hanna? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took us to a desert, and they spilled the load in the desert. And I was with a girl by name, Anna. In that case, she was-- she had a uncle to come and pick her up. But the rest of us had nobody to pick us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, what do you call those animals-- they cry at night? Coyotes? They were crying, and I was so scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was-- the British had these aluminum-covered shacks or something, where they stayed. I don't know the name. And when those cried-- and I was very scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Anne went to snoop out. And there was a-- a woman there, and she let us in. Because I was afraid to be outdoors at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed there. And Anne said, I don't care. My-- my uncle will come and take me home. In those years in Israel, you couldn't be on the road. Because they'll come and pick you up as spies.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- in Pardes Hanna, there is a place that I had my mother's cousin. And they had-- how do you say in English [NON-ENGLISH]? They had those oranges and grapefruits-- those things. And we-- now-- we met in Marseilles a boy who told me-- I didn't have money. And he lent me $5 that time. And I said to-- as soon as I come to Israel, I borrow money from my relatives there, and I'll give it back to you. And my sister went a week before me there. She went, and she got to find nurses that we knew from the front. They live now in-- forgot the name. And she knew where to go-- not to go to them, but to find employment. And she left in next week. Next Saturday night, I came there. And in the desert, there was this boy. And I-- he said, I know where your sister is. I'll get you there. And he took me there to those relatives. And she was there. But there is no point of being there. We didn't come to sit there. We came to look for the nurses that we know from before and look for employment. In meantime I was in a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --what do you call it? Where all the-- well, I'll call them refugees. Was I a refugee or what? Call it something like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was this man-- nasty as could be. And I told him, now-- you see they get the provision when you weren't there. And I said to him, now, you keep my card. I am going just to find out where I can be, and I'll come back. When-- and he betrayed me. And I come back, and I had trouble. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oh, I forgot. When I came to Israel, they had my things kept in the port. They wouldn't give it to me. I brought in friends. They used to say, in Israel, they don't have gas. It's only electric. So I bought in-- in-- in fact, a boy that I knew, he lend me some money, and I bought those electric plates.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Let's go back to Israel, though. So you found your relatives. And did you go to work in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had my Polish papers that I am a nurse. And first, they put me into a city clinic. And I worked there a 1/2 year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that? Do you remember the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tel Aviv, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked in the-- in the clinic in Tel Aviv. And then where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, I went to look for this friend from the military time. And I worked in Haifa. Besides, in Haifa, I had a friend whom-- and she just died-- who we knew from sixth grade, in-- in my hometown. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you marry in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --wait a minute. I had left my-- my husband in Poland. And he was operated for the kidneys or something. And I asked a Polish friend, come and visit him-- if he needs something. He was right there operated when I had to leave. Then he came to Israel. And then we had to marry again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked in Jaffa-- in Jaffa-- in a clinic-- not far from where-- when you come to Israel, where do you live? So my husband had a-- in the-- second husband-- he had a-- a friend from Galicia. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those years you had to pay a-- you see, when they came from the front, those people, from the Jewish military, they used to give them places. So my husband paid him for that room. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What a devilish family. Were you in Israel? Were you in Palestine when Israel became a state?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to Israel, in 1948, I think the-- was the rest of the fights, like the end of it. Because the Arabs said, they going to put all of them into the sea! But they had, in the desert, training people for the [NON-ENGLISH]. He told you before-- in another group-- I forgot now the name. So these gave the British a big beating, and they ran off.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Close to eight years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you decide to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I decided to leave before I even went there. That was the only place I could go, according to Stalin. [NON-ENGLISH] So then I stayed there extra time, waiting for the visa-- and then 19-- I think 1954, when I finally arrived in New York port-- I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came with you to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came by yourself? Your sister did not come with you or your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was already here. She came to the port to-- to pick me up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you started out in New York City?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I started to work in a infant's home of Brooklyn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I get there? There was a-- in New York, an institution called Self-- Self Help for Immigrants of Central Europe. And I went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said to me, there is a mother who has four children. But she is sick, and she has to stay there. She cannot get home. But when she will get home, you will have to help her with the newborn baby-- with that baby. That's how I went there. When I came there, she was still in the hospital. And the husband had hands fulls to-- to him-- how to handle those children. One was 10-years-old-- a bad girl. The other one was 7. The other one was 5. The other one was 2 and 1/2 And I had to start to train them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the father said, so what? When she makes in her pants, so take it and put it on the balcony. I said that's you. Or are you handle them, or you go to work, and I'll handle them. So, I said to her-- I forgot her name. Now-- he said, when she doesn't want to go to school, just let her. I say, that's you. When I'm here, I'll do. How long did you stay with this family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to school to get licensed in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going, as I told you, I had these cassettes from Queen of Angels, from Howard Presbyterian Hospital. I still have the cassettes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you move to Los Angeles-- from New York?

speaker: interviewer
text: What brought you to Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- I had a friend. I don't know if she's alive or not. And her, she had, in Los Angeles, cousins of her killed husband. And she was remarried. And-- and they said, if your husband-- if you want to come here, we'll find some employment for him. He wound up to clean carpets. And-- and she-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --was going to school in the evening with me. My sister lived across from the school. So I would leave the infants home, and take my bundles, and stay in my sister's overnight. And next morning, I go to school-- or the other way around-- something like this. That's how I learned English. And when I came to-- to English class, there in New York, the teacher said, English is a crazy language, but it's up to you to know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Did your sister come to Los Angeles with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was before me two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister keeps going places in front of you. [LAUGHS] So you've had you and your sister here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have other family here?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. On the East coast, yes, but here, nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you practiced--

speaker: interviewee
text: There is some relative somewheres in Los Angeles on my father's side, but I even forgot his name. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've practiced nursing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still work as a nurse?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked until 40 years. And then in 19-- no-- 1937 to 1977. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to Social Security for whatever you call it-- benefits or something? And the woman said, if you would stay a little longer, you lose out a lot of your benefits. And I said, I cannot go on another day. I'd rather take it now or die later. So that's how it happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now the Germans give me a pension for what they did to us. So I have Social Security-- that restitution money from the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Based on your experiences, which have certainly been tremendous, is there anything you'd like to say in closing that when future generations look at this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I only cannot say if-- what to do with. I can wish for them. What to do? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're in a situation they wouldn't have to go through any of this at all. They're in what you call, in a free land. Even Israel is not so free.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what can I tell them? I would tell them, improve your mind and physical as much as you can. Study until your mind can't allow you to study-- until you can't. That I can tell them. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Your welcome, 'cause I went through mills until I got where I am now.
